readingShort.title: Email: English Camp Sign-Up
readingShort.passage:
From: Ms. Pranee Wong, English Club Coordinator
To: All English Club Members
Subject: English Camp — Important Details and Sign-Up

Dear members,

I am pleased to announce that our annual English Camp will take place on Saturday 15 March at the school hall, from 9 a.m. to 4 p.m. This year's theme is "Storytelling Through Drama," and we are lucky to have a guest speaker from Khon Kaen University leading the afternoon workshop.

Please note the following points:
- The camp is free, but places are limited to forty students.
- To reserve a place, reply to this email by Friday 7 March.
- Bring a packed lunch; drinks and snacks will be provided.
- Wear comfortable clothes, as some activities involve movement.

If you have already signed up for the debate competition, you may still join the camp, but please tell me in advance so that I can arrange the schedule.

I look forward to seeing many of you there. Feel free to contact me if you have any questions.

Best regards,
Ms. Pranee Wong
English Club Coordinator

questions.stem: What is the main purpose of this email?
options: To announce an English Camp and explain how to join.
To cancel the school's annual English Camp this year.
To invite members to a debate competition next week.
To report on the results of last year's English Camp.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ A — อีเมลประกาศการจัด English Camp และบอกวิธีสมัคร (reply by Friday 7 March) ส่วน B/C/D ขัดกับเนื้อหา (ไม่ได้ยกเลิก ไม่ใช่การแข่ง debate และไม่ได้รายงานผลปีก่อน)

questions.stem: How can a student reserve a place at the camp?
options: By paying a small fee at the school office.
By calling the guest speaker before the camp.
By replying to the email before Friday 7 March.
By signing a form at the hall on the camp day.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ C — อีเมลระบุว่า "To reserve a place, reply to this email by Friday 7 March" ส่วนค่ายนี้ฟรี (ไม่ต้องจ่าย) จึงตัดข้ออื่นออก

questions.stem: What are students asked to bring to the camp?
options: Their own bottles of water and snacks.
A packed lunch for the middle of the day.
A laptop computer for the drama workshop.
A ticket bought from the coordinator first.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ B — อีเมลบอกว่า "Bring a packed lunch; drinks and snacks will be provided" ดังนั้นต้องเตรียมอาหารกลางวันมาเอง ส่วนเครื่องดื่มและของว่างทางค่ายจัดให้

questions.stem: What can be inferred about the English Camp?
options: It is expected to attract very few students.
It will be held outdoors in the school garden.
It is open only to students who can pay a fee.
It is popular, so places may fill up quickly.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ D — ค่ายฟรีแต่ "places are limited to forty" และให้รีบ reply ภายในวันที่กำหนด จึงอนุมานได้ว่าเป็นที่นิยมและที่นั่งอาจเต็มเร็ว

questions.stem: In the email, the word "reserve" is closest in meaning to:
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ C — "reserve a place" หมายถึงจองที่ไว้ล่วงหน้า ตรงกับ "to book ahead" ไม่ใช่คืน จ่ายเงิน หรือบรรยาย

questions.stem: How would you best describe the tone of the email?
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ A — ผู้เขียนใช้ภาษาสุภาพเป็นกันเอง (Dear members, I look forward to seeing you, Feel free to contact me) พร้อมให้ข้อมูลชัดเจน จึงเป็นน้ำเสียงเป็นมิตรและให้ข้อมูล

readingShort.title: Notice: Library Hours During Exams
readingShort.passage:
NOTICE — School Library

To all students,

During the final examination period (1–14 October), the library will extend its opening hours to help you prepare.

New opening hours:
- Monday to Friday: 7 a.m. – 8 p.m.
- Saturday: 9 a.m. – 5 p.m.
- Sunday: closed

Please remember the following rules:
- Keep your voice low. The study zone on the second floor is for silent study only.
- You may borrow up to five books at a time during this period.
- Food is not allowed inside, but covered water bottles are fine.

Students who return books late will not be able to borrow again until the fine has been paid.

We wish you the very best of luck in your exams.

The Library Team

questions.stem: What is the main purpose of this notice?
options: To announce that the library will close for exams.
To remind students to return all their library books.
To inform students of extended hours during exams.
To introduce a new study zone on the first floor.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ C — ประกาศแจ้งว่าห้องสมุด "extend its opening hours" ช่วงสอบ ส่วน A ตรงข้ามกับเนื้อหา, B/D เป็นรายละเอียดเล็กน้อยหรือไม่ตรง

questions.stem: When is the library open on Saturdays during this period?
options: From 9 a.m. until 5 p.m. in the afternoon.
From 7 a.m. until 8 p.m. in the evening.
It stays completely closed all of Saturday.
From 9 a.m. until 8 p.m. without any break.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ A — ประกาศระบุ "Saturday: 9 a.m. – 5 p.m." ส่วน 7–20 น. คือจันทร์–ศุกร์ และวันอาทิตย์ปิด จึงตัดข้ออื่นออก

questions.stem: What does the notice say about the second-floor study zone?
options: It is reserved only for teachers during exams.
It is the place where students may eat lunch.
It is closed throughout the whole exam period.
It is for silent study, so voices must be low.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ D — ประกาศบอกว่าโซนชั้นสองเป็น "silent study only" และให้เก็บเสียงเบา ตัวเลือกอื่นไม่มีในประกาศ

questions.stem: What will happen to a student who returns books late?
options: They will be banned from the library forever.
They cannot borrow again until the fine is paid.
They must pay double the price of the books.
They will be reported to their own class teacher.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ B — ประกาศระบุว่าคนคืนหนังสือช้า "will not be able to borrow again until the fine has been paid" ตัวเลือกอื่นเกินจริงหรือไม่มีในบท

questions.stem: In the notice, the word "extend" is closest in meaning to:
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ C — "extend its hours" หมายถึงขยาย/ทำให้นานขึ้น ตรงกับ "to make longer" ไม่ใช่ตัดให้สั้นหรือปิด

questions.stem: How would you describe the tone of the notice?
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ A — ประกาศช่วยอำนวยความสะดวก (ขยายเวลา) และอวยพร "best of luck" จึงเป็นน้ำเสียงช่วยเหลือและให้กำลังใจ

readingShort.title: How to Set Up Your Student Email Account
readingShort.passage:
Follow these steps to set up your school email account before the first day of class.

1. Open your web browser and go to the school website.
2. Click the "Student Login" button at the top right of the page.
3. Enter the username printed on your registration card. Your first password is your date of birth in the form DDMMYYYY.
4. The system will then ask you to create a new password. Choose one that is at least eight characters long and includes a number.
5. Once you are logged in, read the "Welcome" message, which contains your class timetable.

If you forget your password, do not create a second account. Instead, visit the IT office in Room 105 with your student card, and a staff member will reset it for you.

questions.stem: What is the main purpose of this text?
options: To explain how to repair a broken school computer.
To explain how to set up a student email account.
To explain how to register for brand-new classes.
To explain how to contact a teacher by email.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ B — ข้อความเป็นขั้นตอนการตั้งค่าอีเมลนักเรียน (set up your school email account) ตัวเลือกอื่นไม่ตรงกับเนื้อหา

questions.stem: What is a student's first password?
options: The username printed on the registration card.
A word chosen freely by the student in advance.
A secret code sent later by the IT office staff.
The student's date of birth in DDMMYYYY form.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ D — ขั้นที่ 3 ระบุว่ารหัสผ่านแรกคือวันเกิดรูปแบบ DDMMYYYY ส่วน username ใช้ล็อกอิน ไม่ใช่รหัสผ่าน

questions.stem: What must the new password include?
options: At least eight characters and a number.
At least four letters and one symbol.
The student's full name and birth year.
A capital letter and the word school.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ A — ขั้นที่ 4 บอกว่ารหัสใหม่ต้องยาวอย่างน้อย 8 ตัวอักษรและมีตัวเลข ตัวเลือกอื่นไม่ตรงเงื่อนไข

questions.stem: What should a student do if they forget the password?
options: Quickly create a brand-new second account.
Send an email straight to the headmaster.
Visit the IT office with a student card.
Wait for the system to reset it on its own.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ C — ข้อความบอกว่าอย่าสร้างบัญชีที่สอง ให้ไปที่ IT office ห้อง 105 พร้อมบัตรนักเรียน ตัวเลือกอื่นขัดกับคำแนะนำ

questions.stem: In the text, the word "reset" is closest in meaning to:
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ B — "reset the password" คือตั้งรหัสใหม่ให้ ตรงกับ "to set again" ไม่ใช่ลบทิ้ง พิมพ์ หรือล็อก

questions.stem: How is the information mainly organized?
options: As a list of rules in order of importance.
As a comparison between two email systems.
As a story about one student's first day.
As a series of steps in the order to follow.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ D — ข้อความเรียงเป็นขั้นตอน 1–5 ตามลำดับที่ต้องทำ จึงเป็นการจัดเรียงแบบขั้นตอน ไม่ใช่เปรียบเทียบหรือเล่าเรื่อง

readingShort.title: The Rise of Urban Farming
readingShort.passage:
In cities around the world, a quiet change is taking place on rooftops, balconies, and empty plots of land. Urban farming — growing food within a city rather than in the countryside — is becoming increasingly popular, and for several practical reasons.

The first is freshness. Vegetables grown in the city can reach local markets within hours, rather than travelling long distances by truck. This not only improves their taste but also reduces the fuel used in transport.

Urban farms also make use of space that would otherwise be wasted. A flat rooftop, for example, can be turned into a productive garden that helps cool the building below. Some restaurants now grow herbs on their own roofs, picking them only minutes before they are served.

There are social benefits too. Community gardens, where neighbours share a plot of land, bring people together and give city children a chance to see how food is grown. For many families, this connection to nature is a welcome break from busy urban life.

Of course, urban farming cannot replace traditional agriculture; cities simply do not have enough space to feed everyone. But as a supplement, it offers fresher food, greener buildings, and stronger communities — a small but meaningful step toward more sustainable cities.

questions.stem: What is the passage mainly about?
options: Why traditional farming is no longer necessary.
How to grow vegetables on a small balcony.
The benefits of farming inside modern cities.
The long history of farming in the countryside.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ C — บทเล่าข้อดีของการทำเกษตรในเมือง (urban farming) ส่วน A ขัดกับย่อหน้าสุดท้าย, B/D เป็นรายละเอียดหรือไม่ใช่ใจความหลัก

questions.stem: According to the passage, why does city-grown food taste better?
options: It reaches local markets within just a few hours.
It is grown using special chemical fertilisers.
It is always sold at much cheaper market prices.
It is kept in cold storage for several days first.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ A — ย่อหน้าที่ 2 ระบุว่าผักในเมืองถึงตลาดภายในไม่กี่ชั่วโมง ทำให้รสชาติดีขึ้น ตัวเลือกอื่นไม่มีในบท

questions.stem: How can a rooftop garden help the building below it?
options: By providing several extra rooms for residents.
By helping to keep the building cooler.
By blocking all the noise from the street.
By producing electricity from the sunlight.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ B — ย่อหน้าที่ 3 บอกว่าสวนบนดาดฟ้า "helps cool the building below" ตัวเลือกอื่นไม่ได้กล่าวถึง

questions.stem: In the last paragraph, the word "supplement" is closest in meaning to:
options: a strict rule that must be obeyed
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ D — "as a supplement" หมายถึงสิ่งที่เสริมเข้าไป ตรงกับ "an extra addition" บริบทคือเกษตรในเมืองเป็นส่วนเสริม ไม่ใช่แทนที่เกษตรปกติ

questions.stem: In paragraph 4, "this connection" refers to:
options: the link between two separate city farms
the bond formed between teachers and pupils
city children seeing how food is grown
the route that delivery trucks take daily
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ C — ประโยคก่อนหน้าพูดถึงเด็กในเมืองได้เห็นว่าอาหารปลูกอย่างไร ดังนั้น this connection to nature จึงหมายถึงการได้สัมผัสธรรมชาติผ่านการเห็นการปลูกอาหาร

questions.stem: What does the writer suggest about urban farming?
options: It is useful but cannot feed a whole city alone.
It will soon completely replace normal farms.
It is far too expensive for most cities to try.
It can only ever work in very small villages.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ A — ย่อหน้าสุดท้ายบอกว่ามันแทนเกษตรดั้งเดิมไม่ได้ แต่เป็นส่วนเสริมที่ดี จึงอนุมานได้ว่ามีประโยชน์แต่เลี้ยงคนทั้งเมืองลำพังไม่ได้

questions.stem: What is the writer's attitude toward urban farming?
options: Positive but realistic about its limits.
Confused about how it really works.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ B — ผู้เขียนชมข้อดีหลายอย่างแต่ยอมรับข้อจำกัด (แทนเกษตรปกติไม่ได้) จึงเป็นทัศนคติเชิงบวกแต่สมจริง

readingShort.title: Should Students Use Smartphones in Class?
readingShort.passage:
Few topics divide teachers and students as sharply as the use of smartphones in the classroom. Some schools ban them completely, while others encourage their use as learning tools. So which side is right?

Those who oppose phones in class make a strong case. Smartphones are a constant source of distraction: a single notification can pull a student's attention away from a lesson, and research suggests that even having a phone on the desk can reduce concentration. There is also the temptation to cheat, or simply to scroll through social media instead of listening.

Supporters, however, argue that banning phones ignores their real value. A smartphone can be a dictionary, a calculator, and a gateway to information all at once. Used well, it allows students to look up unfamiliar words, take part in online quizzes, and learn at their own pace.

The truth, perhaps, lies somewhere in between. The problem is not the device itself but how it is used. Rather than banning phones outright, schools might teach students to use them responsibly — switching them to silent during explanations, and taking them out only for specific tasks. In a world where technology is everywhere, learning to manage it may prove more useful than learning to live without it.

questions.stem: What is the main question the writer explores?
options: Whether smartphones are too costly for students.
Whether teachers should be allowed to use phones.
Whether social media is harmful to young people.
Whether students should use smartphones in class.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ D — ทั้งบทถกประเด็น "ควรให้นักเรียนใช้สมาร์ตโฟนในห้องเรียนไหม" ตัวเลือกอื่นเป็นประเด็นข้างเคียงที่ไม่ใช่คำถามหลัก

questions.stem: What argument do those who oppose phones make?
options: Phones are a constant source of distraction.
Phones are far too heavy to carry around.
Phones are useful dictionaries and calculators.
Phones help students learn at their own pace.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ A — ฝ่ายค้านชี้ว่าโทรศัพท์ "a constant source of distraction" และลดสมาธิ ส่วน C/D เป็นข้อโต้แย้งของฝ่ายสนับสนุน

questions.stem: How do supporters say a smartphone can help learning?
options: By replacing the teacher in every single lesson.
By keeping all students silent during the class.
By acting as a dictionary and a calculator.
By completely preventing any online cheating.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ C — ฝ่ายสนับสนุนบอกว่าโทรศัพท์เป็นทั้งพจนานุกรม เครื่องคิดเลข และประตูสู่ข้อมูล ตัวเลือกอื่นเกินจริงหรือไม่มีในบท

questions.stem: In paragraph 4, the word "outright" is closest in meaning to:
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ B — "banning phones outright" หมายถึงสั่งห้ามแบบเด็ดขาดทันที ตรงกับ "completely and at once"

questions.stem: What is the writer's own view by the end of the passage?
options: Phones should be banned from every classroom.
Phones should be used freely with no limits.
The real issue is how phones are used.
Teachers should never carry phones at all.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ C — ย่อหน้าสุดท้ายสรุปว่า "The problem is not the device itself but how it is used" จึงมองว่าประเด็นอยู่ที่วิธีใช้ ไม่ใช่ห้ามหรือปล่อยเสรี

questions.stem: How would you best describe the writer's approach?
options: Balanced, weighing both sides before judging.
Joking, treating the topic as a game.
Hopeless, sure nothing can be done.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ A — ผู้เขียนเสนอทั้งฝ่ายค้านและฝ่ายสนับสนุนก่อนสรุปจุดยืนกลางๆ จึงเป็นแนวสมดุล ชั่งน้ำหนักสองด้าน

questions.stem: How is the passage mainly organized?
options: It lists steps for using a phone in class.
It tells one student's story about a phone.
It compares the prices of several phones.
It gives both sides, then a middle position.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ D — บทเสนอข้อโต้แย้งฝ่ายค้าน → ฝ่ายสนับสนุน → แล้วปิดด้วยจุดยืนตรงกลาง จึงเป็นโครงแบบนี้

readingShort.title: Letter: A Reply to a Customer Complaint
readingShort.passage:
Dear Ms. Chen,

Thank you for your letter of 3 May regarding the laptop bag you purchased from our online store. I am sorry to hear that the zip broke after only two weeks, and I understand how frustrating this must be.

Having looked into your case, I can confirm that your purchase is still covered by our one-year warranty. We would therefore like to offer you two options. You may either return the faulty bag for a full refund, or we can send you a replacement free of charge. In both cases, we will cover the cost of postage.

To proceed, simply reply to this letter and let us know which option you prefer. If you choose a replacement, please confirm your delivery address so that we can send the new bag without delay.

Once again, please accept our apologies for the inconvenience. We value you as a customer and hope to serve you better in the future.

Yours sincerely,
David Lim
Customer Service Manager

questions.stem: What is the main purpose of this letter?
options: To advertise a brand-new range of laptop bags.
To respond to a complaint and offer some options.
To ask the customer to pay for a repair service.
To explain why the warranty has already expired.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ B — จดหมายตอบข้อร้องเรียนเรื่องกระเป๋าเสียและเสนอทางเลือก (คืนเงินหรือเปลี่ยนใหม่) ตัวเลือกอื่นขัดกับเนื้อหา

questions.stem: What two options does the company offer Ms. Chen?
options: A discount voucher or a written apology.
A repair in store or a longer warranty.
A full refund or a free replacement bag.
A cash reward or a free laptop computer.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ C — จดหมายเสนอ "a full refund" หรือ "a replacement free of charge" ตัวเลือกอื่นไม่ตรงกับข้อเสนอ

questions.stem: Who will pay for the cost of postage?
options: The company will cover the postage cost.
Ms. Chen must pay the postage herself.
The postage will be split between them.
No postage is needed for either option.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ A — จดหมายระบุ "we will cover the cost of postage" จึงเป็นบริษัทที่จ่ายค่าส่ง

questions.stem: What can be inferred about the company?
options: It refuses to take responsibility for faults.
It no longer sells any kind of laptop bag.
It blames Ms. Chen for breaking the zip.
It wants to keep the customer satisfied.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ D — บริษัทขอโทษ เสนอทางเลือก และบอกว่า "value you as a customer" จึงอนุมานได้ว่าต้องการรักษาลูกค้าให้พอใจ

questions.stem: In the letter, the word "faulty" is closest in meaning to:
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ B — "the faulty bag" หมายถึงกระเป๋าที่ชำรุด/ใช้ไม่ได้ ตรงกับ "broken or not working" บริบทคือซิปเสีย

questions.stem: How would you describe the tone of the letter?
options: Rude and dismissive of the customer.
Casual and full of friendly jokes.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ C — จดหมายใช้ภาษาสุภาพ ขอโทษ และเป็นทางการ (Dear / Yours sincerely / accept our apologies) จึงเป็นน้ำเสียงสุภาพและเป็นมืออาชีพ

readingShort.title: How Music Shapes the Brain
readingShort.passage:
Most of us listen to music every day — on the way to school, while studying, or simply to relax — without thinking much about what it actually does to us. Yet over the past few decades, scientists have discovered that music is far more than entertainment. It affects the human brain in remarkable ways, shaping our emotions, our memory, our bodies, and even the way we learn.

Perhaps the most obvious effect of music is on our mood. A fast, upbeat song can make us feel energetic, while a slow, gentle melody can calm us down or bring back distant memories. This happens because music triggers the release of chemicals in the brain that are linked to pleasure and reward — the same chemicals involved when we eat a favourite food or receive good news. This is why a single song can sometimes change the way we feel in a matter of seconds.

Music also has a surprisingly powerful connection to memory. Many people find it far easier to remember information when it is set to a tune; this is exactly why young children learn the alphabet through song. Researchers have even found that patients who have lost much of their memory through illness can still recall songs from their youth, long after most other memories have faded. The rhythm and melody seem to provide a kind of map that helps the brain store and later recover information.

Beyond thoughts and feelings, music can even influence the body. Studies have shown that listening to a steady beat can help people walk more smoothly, which is why music is now used to support patients recovering from injuries that affect movement. Hospitals sometimes play calming music to lower patients' stress before an operation, and athletes rely on energetic playlists to push themselves further during training. The body, it seems, responds naturally to rhythm, often without our being aware of it.

Given all these benefits, it is tempting to assume that music always helps us study. The truth, however, is more complicated. Quiet background music without words may help some people concentrate, but loud music with lyrics often does the opposite, competing for the very attention that the task requires. Whether music helps or harms, in other words, depends on the person, the type of music, and the kind of work being done.

What is clear is that music is deeply woven into the way our minds and bodies work. It can lift our spirits, unlock distant memories, ease pain, and, used wisely, support our learning. The next time you put on your headphones, it may be worth remembering that you are not simply hearing a song — you are giving your brain a gentle but genuine workout.

questions.stem: What is the passage mainly about?

readingLong.title: Should Single-Use Plastics Be Banned?
readingLong.passage:
Plastic bags, straws, bottles, and food containers have become part of daily life. They are cheap, light, and convenient — and they are almost everywhere. In recent years, however, many governments have begun to ban or tax single-use plastics: items that are designed to be thrown away after only one use. Supporters say such measures are long overdue, while critics argue that they cause more problems than they solve. So which side has the stronger case?

Those in favour of banning single-use plastics point to the enormous damage these products cause. The great majority of them are never recycled. Instead, they end up in rivers, oceans, and landfills, where they can take hundreds of years to break down. Along the way, they harm wildlife: sea turtles mistake floating plastic bags for jellyfish, and seabirds feed tiny plastic fragments to their chicks. Every year, millions of tonnes of plastic flow into the sea, and much of it never truly disappears — it only breaks into smaller and smaller pieces. By cutting plastic off at the source, supporters argue, a ban deals with the problem before it begins, rather than relying on recycling systems that frequently fail.

Opponents, however, raise a number of practical concerns. Plastic is cheap, light, and hygienic, and for some people it is genuinely hard to replace. Disabled people, for instance, may depend on flexible plastic straws that stiff paper ones cannot match. Small businesses worry about the rising cost of alternatives, and some scientists point out that producing paper or cotton bags can actually use more water and energy than making plastic ones. A poorly designed ban, they warn, may simply move the harm somewhere else rather than removing it.

Both sides make fair points, and the issue is rarely as simple as it first appears. A ban that removes plastic straws but ignores the far larger problem of plastic packaging may achieve very little in practice. On the other hand, doing nothing at all is clearly not a serious option, given the sheer scale of plastic pollution today. The wisest path may lie in careful design: banning the most harmful and least necessary items first, while offering affordable alternatives and protecting the small number of people who truly need certain plastic products.

In the end, single-use plastics offer a useful example of how environmental decisions are seldom black and white. Banning them can bring real and lasting benefits, but only if it is done thoughtfully, with proper attention to cost, fairness, and the wider picture. The aim, after all, is not merely to be seen to be doing something, but to make a genuine difference to the planet that we all share.

questions.stem: What is the main question the passage explores?
options: How plastic bottles and bags are made in factories.
Why plastic is cheaper than paper and cotton bags.

questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ C — ทั้งบทถกว่าควรแบนพลาสติกใช้ครั้งเดียวหรือไม่ ตัวเลือกอื่นเป็นรายละเอียดย่อยหรือไม่ใช่ประเด็นหลัก

questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ A — ย่อหน้าที่ 2 บอกว่าพลาสติกส่วนใหญ่ไม่ถูกรีไซเคิลและใช้เวลาย่อยสลายหลายร้อยปี ตัวเลือกอื่นไม่ใช่เหตุผลฝ่ายสนับสนุน

readingLong.title: Why We Procrastinate
readingLong.passage:
Almost everyone has done it. A deadline is approaching, an important task is waiting, and yet we find ourselves tidying a desk, checking a phone, or watching just one more video instead. Procrastination — the habit of needlessly delaying what we know we should do — is one of the most common of human weaknesses. What makes it so puzzling is that we often procrastinate even when we fully understand that it will cost us dearly later. Why do intelligent, capable people so regularly act against their own interests?

For a long time, procrastination was dismissed as simple laziness or weak willpower. More recent research, however, suggests something far more interesting. Psychologists now argue that procrastination is less about managing time than about managing emotions. When a task makes us feel anxious, bored, or unsure of ourselves, putting it off offers instant relief. We are not really avoiding the work itself so much as avoiding the unpleasant feelings attached to it.

This helps explain why procrastination can feel so pleasant in the moment and so painful afterwards. By delaying a difficult task, we escape discomfort now, but we quietly hand a larger problem to our future selves. The relief we gain is genuine but temporary; the task does not vanish, and it usually grows more stressful as the deadline draws nearer. In this sense, procrastination behaves like a trap that rewards us immediately while punishing us in the long run.

Part of the explanation lies in the way the human brain weighs time. We tend to value rewards that we can enjoy right now far more highly than benefits that lie somewhere in the future — a tendency that psychologists call "present bias." The discomfort of beginning a hard task is immediate and vivid, while the satisfaction of completing it feels distant and abstract. Faced with this imbalance, the brain naturally leans towards the easier and more comfortable option in front of it.

Seeing procrastination as an emotional habit, rather than a simple character flaw, points the way towards better solutions. Instead of merely ordering ourselves to "try harder," we can make the first step smaller and less frightening — promising to work for just five minutes, for example, or breaking an enormous task into smaller, manageable pieces. Interestingly, research also suggests that forgiving ourselves for past delays makes us less likely to procrastinate again, because it removes some of the very anxiety that caused the problem in the first place.

Procrastination, then, is not really a sign that we are lazy or foolish. It is a deeply human reaction to uncomfortable feelings, and like most habits, it can be understood and slowly changed. The next time you catch yourself delaying, the most useful question may not be "Why am I so lazy?" but rather "What is it about this task that I am trying to avoid?" — and that small shift in thinking can make a surprising difference.

questions.stem: What is the main idea of the passage?

questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ C — แก่นของบทคือ การผัดวันประกันพรุ่งเป็นนิสัยทางอารมณ์ที่เข้าใจและค่อยๆ แก้ได้ ส่วน A/B ขัดกับงานวิจัยที่บทเสนอ และ D ไม่ใช่สาระ

questions.stem: How would you best describe the writer's tone?

questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ B — ผู้เขียนอธิบายอย่างเข้าอกเข้าใจ ไม่ตัดสินว่าขี้เกียจ และเสนอทางออกเชิงให้กำลังใจ จึงเป็นน้ำเสียงเข้าใจและให้กำลังใจ

readingLong.title: How Bees Talk to Each Other
readingLong.passage:
When a honeybee discovers a rich source of flowers, it faces an interesting problem: how can it tell the thousands of other bees in its hive exactly where the food is? Bees cannot speak, draw maps, or point. Yet somehow, within minutes, dozens of bees fly straight to a flower patch that may be hundreds of metres away. For a long time this was a mystery, until scientists discovered that bees communicate through an extraordinary form of movement known as the "waggle dance."

The dance takes place inside the dark hive, on the vertical surface of the honeycomb. A returning bee walks in a straight line while rapidly shaking its body from side to side, then circles back to repeat the movement. This simple-looking dance actually carries two crucial pieces of information: direction and distance. The angle of the straight run, measured against the vertical, tells the other bees the direction of the food in relation to the sun. The length of the run, and the number of waggles, indicates how far away the flowers are.

What makes this remarkable is that the other bees cannot see the dance in the darkness of the hive. Instead, they crowd around the dancer and feel her movements and vibrations with their antennae and bodies. In this way, a message is passed from one insect to many, without a single sound or image.

Scientists have tested the accuracy of the dance by tracking where bees fly after watching it. The results are striking: the watchers travel in the direction and for the distance that the dance predicts, often arriving very close to the original flowers. Mistakes do happen, especially when the food source is very close to the hive, but the system is impressively reliable for creatures with such tiny brains. Even cloudy days do not stop the bees, because they can sense the position of the sun through patterns in the light that human eyes cannot detect.

The waggle dance raises deep questions about animal intelligence. We often assume that complex communication requires a large brain or even language as humans know it. Bees suggest otherwise. Their dance is a kind of code, shaped over millions of years of evolution, that allows a colony to share knowledge and act together. The dance has also inspired scientists in surprising fields, from robotics to the design of systems that help groups of machines share information. Studying it not only reveals the hidden lives of insects but also reminds us that intelligence in nature can take forms very different from our own, hidden in creatures we might otherwise overlook.

questions.stem: What is the passage mainly about?
options: The way honeybees use a special dance to share information.
The reason honeybees build their hives in dark places.
The kinds of bright flowers that honeybees most prefer to visit each day.

questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ A — ทั้งบทอธิบายว่าผึ้งใช้ "waggle dance" สื่อสารบอกทิศทางและระยะทางของอาหาร ส่วน B/C/D เป็นรายละเอียดเล็กน้อยหรือไม่ได้กล่าวถึง

questions.stem: What two pieces of information does the waggle dance carry?

questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ B — ย่อหน้าที่ 2 ระบุว่าการเต้นบอกสองสิ่งคือ "direction and distance" ของอาหาร ตัวเลือกอื่นไม่ใช่ข้อมูลที่บทกล่าวถึง

questions.stem: How do other bees receive the message in the dark hive?
options: By watching the dancer with their large eyes.
By listening to the sounds the dancer makes.
By feeling the dancer's movements and vibrations.

questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ C — ย่อหน้าที่ 3 บอกว่าผึ้งตัวอื่นมองไม่เห็นในความมืด แต่ "feel her movements and vibrations" ด้วยหนวดและลำตัว

questions.stem: In paragraph 4, the word "striking" is closest in meaning to:
options: quite ordinary and not at all unusual
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ D — "the results are striking" หมายถึงผลลัพธ์ที่น่าทึ่ง/ชัดเจน ตรงกับ "remarkable and clear" บริบทคือผึ้งบินไปได้แม่นยำตามที่การเต้นบอก

questions.stem: In paragraph 3, "a single sound or image" describes what the dance works without. This emphasizes that bees communicate:
options: only when the sun is shining brightly
by making very loud buzzing noises
using maps drawn on the honeycomb
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ B — ประโยคเน้นว่าสารถูกส่งต่อ "without a single sound or image" จึงเน้นว่าผึ้งสื่อสารโดยไม่ต้องใช้เสียงหรือภาพ สอดคล้องกับต้นบทที่บอกว่าผึ้งพูดหรือวาดแผนที่ไม่ได้

questions.stem: What does the waggle dance suggest about intelligence in nature?
options: Only animals with large brains can communicate well.
Complex communication can exist without a large brain.
Insects are actually smarter than most large animals.
Animal communication is always far less accurate than ordinary human speech is.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ B — ย่อหน้าสุดท้ายบอกว่าผึ้งแสดงให้เห็นว่าการสื่อสารที่ซับซ้อนไม่จำเป็นต้องมีสมองใหญ่ จึงอนุมานได้ตามข้อ B ส่วน C/D เกินจากที่บทกล่าว

questions.stem: What is the writer's main purpose?
options: To warn readers that bees are dangerous insects.
To teach readers how to keep bees for honey.
To explain a fascinating way that bees communicate.
To argue that bees are smarter than human beings.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ C — บทมุ่งอธิบายวิธีสื่อสารอันน่าทึ่งของผึ้ง ไม่ได้เตือนภัย สอนเลี้ยงผึ้ง หรือเถียงว่าผึ้งฉลาดกว่าคน

questions.stem: How is the passage mainly organized?
options: It poses a puzzle, then explains how it is solved.
It lists the daily activities of a single bee.
It compares two completely different insects.
It gives step-by-step instructions for beekeeping.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ A — บทเริ่มด้วยปริศนา (ผึ้งบอกตำแหน่งอาหารได้อย่างไร) แล้วค่อยอธิบายคำตอบ (waggle dance) จึงเป็นโครงปริศนา–คำอธิบาย

readingLong.title: The Science of First Impressions
readingLong.passage:
It takes only a fraction of a second. The moment we meet someone new, our brain begins forming an opinion — about whether the person is friendly or cold, trustworthy or suspicious, confident or nervous. Research suggests that these first impressions form in as little as a tenth of a second, long before we have heard the person say anything meaningful. What is more surprising is how powerfully these snap judgements shape what follows.

From an evolutionary point of view, the speed of first impressions makes sense. For our distant ancestors, the ability to decide quickly whether a stranger was a friend or a threat could mean the difference between safety and danger. There was no time for careful analysis. A brain that judged fast, even if it was sometimes wrong, was more likely to keep its owner alive than one that hesitated. We have inherited this rapid judging system, even though most of us now live in a world where instant threats are rare.

The problem is that first impressions are often inaccurate. A person who appears cold may simply be shy; someone who seems confident may be hiding deep uncertainty. Yet once an impression has formed, it tends to stick. Psychologists call this the "halo effect": if we decide someone is likeable, we unconsciously assume they are also intelligent, honest, and capable, even without evidence. The reverse is also true. A single negative impression can colour everything we later notice about a person, as we unconsciously search for details that confirm what we already believe.

This matters in real life far more than we might think. Job interviews, first dates, and business meetings are all shaped by impressions formed in seconds. Studies show that interviewers often decide about a candidate within the first few minutes, then spend the rest of the interview looking for reasons to justify that early decision.

Understanding how first impressions work does not free us from them entirely, but it can make us wiser. If we know that our snap judgements are fast, powerful, and frequently mistaken, we can learn to hold them more lightly — to give people a second chance, to question our instant reactions, and to remember that the truth about a person usually takes far longer than a tenth of a second to discover.

questions.stem: What is the passage mainly about?
options: How first impressions form, why they persist, and why they mislead.
How to choose and wear the right clothes for an important job interview.
Why our ancestors lived in such dangerous conditions.
How the brain controls the movement of our bodies.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ A — บทอธิบายว่าความประทับใจแรกเกิดเร็วอย่างไร ทำไมจึงติดทน และทำไมมักผิดพลาด ส่วน B/C/D เป็นรายละเอียดหรือไม่ใช่ใจความหลัก

questions.stem: Why does the passage say fast first impressions made sense for our ancestors?
options: Because they had a great deal of free time.
Because deciding quickly could keep them safe.
Because they enjoyed meeting many new people.
Because they could not see other people clearly.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ B — ย่อหน้าที่ 2 บอกว่าการตัดสินเร็วว่าคนแปลกหน้าเป็นมิตรหรือภัย ช่วยให้บรรพบุรุษอยู่รอด

questions.stem: What is the "halo effect" described in the passage?
options: Assuming a likeable person is also smart and honest.
Forgetting a person's face soon after meeting them.
Judging people only after knowing them for years.
Believing that all strangers are dangerous threats.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ A — ย่อหน้าที่ 3 อธิบายว่า halo effect คือถ้าเราคิดว่าใครน่าชอบ เราจะเหมาเอาว่าเขาฉลาด ซื่อสัตย์ และเก่งด้วยโดยไม่มีหลักฐาน

questions.stem: In paragraph 3, the word "stick" is closest in meaning to:

questions.stem: What can be inferred about job interviews from the passage?
options: Interviewers completely ignore their own first impressions of a candidate.

questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ B — ย่อหน้าที่ 4 บอกว่าผู้สัมภาษณ์มักตัดสินในไม่กี่นาทีแรก แล้วหาเหตุผลมาสนับสนุน จึงอนุมานได้ว่าความประทับใจแรกมีผลต่อทั้งการสัมภาษณ์อย่างไม่เป็นธรรม

questions.stem: In paragraph 3, "The reverse is also true" means that:

a bad impression makes us notice bad things

questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ B — ก่อนหน้าพูดถึง halo effect ด้านบวก (ชอบ→คิดว่าดีไปหมด) ดังนั้น 'the reverse' คือด้านลบ: ความประทับใจแย่ทำให้เรามองหาแต่ข้อเสีย

questions.stem: What does the writer suggest we should do about first impressions?

questions.stem: How is the passage mainly organized?
options: It explains a phenomenon, its origin, its flaws, and a lesson.

It compares the experiments of two different famous scientists.
It gives step-by-step instructions for an interview.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ A — บทอธิบายปรากฏการณ์ (ความประทับใจแรก) → ที่มาเชิงวิวัฒนาการ → ข้อบกพร่อง (มักผิด, halo effect) → ข้อคิด จึงเป็นโครงนี้

readingLong.title: Rice: The Grain That Feeds Half the World
readingLong.passage:
It is easy to take rice for granted. It sits quietly at the centre of the table, the background to almost every meal, so familiar that we hardly notice it. Yet this humble grain feeds more people than any other crop on Earth. More than half of the world's population eats rice every day, and for billions of people in Asia, Africa, and Latin America, a year without rice would be a year of hunger.

Rice is unusual among major crops in the way it is grown. Most varieties spend much of their life standing in water, in the flooded fields known as paddies. The water is not just for the plant's thirst; it also drowns many weeds and pests that would otherwise destroy the harvest, reducing the need for chemicals. This clever system, developed by farmers thousands of years ago, allows the same fields to produce food year after year, sometimes for centuries, without exhausting the soil.

Growing rice has also shaped entire societies. Because flooded fields require careful control of water, rice farming traditionally demanded cooperation: neighbours had to share canals, coordinate planting times, and help each other at harvest. Some historians argue that this need to work together influenced the cultures of rice-growing regions, encouraging strong community ties and shared responsibility. The terraced rice fields carved into hillsides across Asia stand as monuments to generations of collective labour.

Today, however, rice faces serious challenges. The crop is thirsty: producing a single kilogram of rice can require thousands of litres of water, and in many regions water is becoming scarce. Climate change brings further threats, as rising temperatures reduce yields and unpredictable rains disturb planting seasons that farmers have trusted for generations. Meanwhile, the world's population continues to grow, and with it the demand for food.

Scientists are responding with new varieties of rice that need less water, resist disease, and tolerate heat or floods. Farmers are testing techniques that produce more grain with fewer resources, from smarter watering schedules to new ways of preparing the soil. The race is quiet but urgent: the future food security of billions depends on this single plant. The next time a plate of rice appears on the table, it is worth pausing to remember that behind those small white grains lies one of humanity's greatest and most fragile achievements.

questions.stem: What is the passage mainly about?
options: The importance of rice and the challenges it now faces.
The best recipes for cooking rice in different countries.
The reasons why wheat is more popular than rice.
The history of one famous rice farm in Asia.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ A — บทเล่าความสำคัญของข้าว (อาหารครึ่งโลก วิธีปลูก อิทธิพลต่อสังคม) และความท้าทายปัจจุบัน ส่วน B/C/D ไม่ใช่เนื้อหาหลัก

questions.stem: Besides watering the plant, what does flooding the fields do?

questions.stem: How did rice farming influence the cultures of rice-growing regions?

questions.stem: In paragraph 4, the word "scarce" is closest in meaning to:
options: far too cold for anyone to use

questions.stem: Why does the writer call the work of scientists a "quiet but urgent" race?

questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ B — ย่อหน้าสุดท้ายบอกว่า "the future food security of billions depends on this single plant" งานจึงเร่งด่วนแม้ไม่เป็นข่าวดัง

questions.stem: In paragraph 3, "monuments to generations of collective labour" refers to:

questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ B — ประโยคนั้นกล่าวถึง "terraced rice fields carved into hillsides" ว่าเป็นดั่งอนุสรณ์ของแรงงานร่วมหลายชั่วอายุคน

questions.stem: What is the writer's main purpose?
options: To persuade readers that they should stop eating rice altogether.

options: From rice's importance and history to its modern challenges.

readingLong.title: E-Sports: When Gaming Becomes a Career
readingLong.passage:
Twenty years ago, telling your parents that you wanted to play video games for a living would have earned you a worried look and a lecture about real jobs. Today, the picture is very different. Competitive video gaming, known as e-sports, has grown into a global industry worth billions of dollars, with professional players, sold-out stadiums, and audiences that rival traditional sports. The question is no longer whether e-sports is real, but what its rise tells us about work, play, and the modern world.

The scale of the industry surprises many people. Major tournaments offer prize pools worth millions, and the best players earn salaries from professional teams, just as footballers do. Matches are broadcast live to millions of viewers around the world, complete with commentators, coaches, analysts, and team managers, all of whom have built serious careers behind the scenes of the games themselves. Universities in several countries now offer scholarships to talented players, treating gaming skill the way they once treated athletic ability.

Being a professional player, however, is far harder than fans imagine. Top competitors practise eight to twelve hours a day, repeating the same movements and strategies until they become automatic. Careers are short: reaction times begin to slow in a player's mid-twenties, and most professionals retire from competition before the age of thirty. The pressure to perform is intense at every tournament, and burnout is common among even the most successful players. In these ways, e-sports resembles gymnastics or swimming more than an easy life of fun.

The industry also creates opportunities far beyond playing. For every professional player, there are many more jobs in event organisation, video production, marketing, coaching, and software development. Students who love games but lack world-class reflexes can still build careers around them — a point that teachers and parents increasingly recognise.

None of this means that every young gamer should dream of going professional, any more than every school footballer should expect to play in a World Cup. The odds are long, and the sacrifices are real. But the rise of e-sports carries a wider lesson: the line between play and work is being redrawn, and skills once dismissed as wasted time can, in the right setting, become genuinely valuable. The wisest response is neither to worship gaming nor to dismiss it, but to understand it.

questions.stem: What is the passage mainly about?
options: The growth of e-sports and what it means for work and play.
The rules of the most popular video game in the world.
The reasons why parents should ban video games at home.
The history of one famous football team's victories.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ A — บทเล่าการเติบโตของ e-sports เป็นอุตสาหกรรม อาชีพ และบทเรียนที่มันสะท้อนเรื่องงานกับการเล่น ตัวเลือกอื่นไม่ใช่เนื้อหาหลัก

questions.stem: How do universities in several countries treat talented players?

questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ B — ย่อหน้าที่ 2 บอกว่ามหาวิทยาลัยหลายประเทศให้ทุนผู้เล่นที่มีพรสวรรค์ เหมือนที่เคยให้ทุนนักกีฬา

questions.stem: Why are professional gaming careers usually short?

questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ C — ย่อหน้าที่ 3 ระบุว่าปฏิกิริยาตอบสนองเริ่มช้าลงช่วงกลางวัย 20 ปี ผู้เล่นส่วนใหญ่จึงเลิกแข่งก่อนอายุ 30

questions.stem: In paragraph 3, the word "burnout" is closest in meaning to:
options: a sudden victory in a match

questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ B — "burnout" คือภาวะหมดไฟ/อ่อนล้าจากความกดดันและการฝึกหนักต่อเนื่อง ตรงกับ "exhaustion from too much pressure"

questions.stem: Why does the writer compare e-sports to gymnastics or swimming?
options: Because all three sports are played entirely inside the water.
Because all three demand hard training and end early.

questions.stem: In paragraph 4, "a point that teachers and parents increasingly recognise" refers to the idea that:

questions.stem: What is the writer's overall attitude toward gaming?
options: Balanced — neither worshipping nor dismissing it.

questions.stem: How is the passage mainly organized?
options: It shows the industry's scale, its hardships, its wider jobs, then a lesson.
It ranks the world's best players from first to last.

questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ A — บทไล่จากขนาดอุตสาหกรรม → ความยากของอาชีพ → โอกาสงานรอบวงการ → บทเรียนปิดท้าย จึงเป็นโครงนี้

readingLong.title: The Science of Laughter
readingLong.passage:
Laughter is one of the most familiar sounds in the world, yet also one of the strangest. We laugh at jokes, at funny faces, and sometimes at nothing in particular. We laugh when we are happy, but also when we are nervous or embarrassed. Despite how ordinary it seems, scientists have long been puzzled by a simple question: why do human beings laugh at all, and what purpose could such a curious behaviour possibly serve?

One of the first surprises is that laughter is far older than language and far more social than we tend to assume. Studies show that we are up to thirty times more likely to laugh when we are with other people than when we are alone. Most laughter, in fact, has little to do with jokes. It happens during ordinary conversation, marking moments of agreement, friendliness, and shared understanding. In this sense, laughter is less a response to humour than a kind of social glue that quietly binds people together.

Laughter also appears remarkably early in life. Babies begin to laugh at around three or four months old, long before they can speak or understand a single joke. This suggests that the ability to laugh is built into us from birth, rather than something we must be carefully taught. Researchers have even recorded laughter-like sounds in other animals, such as chimpanzees and rats, when they play or are gently tickled, hinting that the roots of laughter reach deep into our evolutionary past.

What happens inside the body during laughter is equally interesting. A good laugh increases our breathing, relaxes our muscles, and triggers the release of chemicals in the brain that are associated with pleasure and with a reduced sense of pain. Some studies suggest that laughter can lower stress and may even strengthen the immune system, although scientists are careful not to exaggerate these effects or promise more than the evidence allows. What is clear is that laughter feels good, and that this good feeling gently encourages us to seek out the company of others.

Perhaps the most important role of laughter is communication. A shared laugh can ease tension, signal that we mean no harm, and turn nervous strangers into comfortable friends. Yet laughter can also wound, as when a group laughs at someone rather than with them. Like language itself, laughter carries meaning, and that meaning depends entirely on the situation in which it occurs.

Far from being a meaningless reflex, then, laughter is a powerful and ancient form of human connection. It reminds us that we are social creatures, shaped by millions of years of living in groups. The next time you laugh with a friend, it is worth remembering that you are taking part in one of the oldest conversations our species has ever known.

questions.stem: What is the passage mainly about?
options: A list of the funniest jokes ever told.
Why humans laugh and what laughter does.
How to become a professional comedian.
The reasons some people never laugh.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ B — ทั้งบทสำรวจว่าทำไมมนุษย์ถึงหัวเราะและการหัวเราะมีบทบาทอะไร ส่วน A/C/D ไม่ใช่ใจความหลัก

questions.stem: How does the amount we laugh change when others are present?
options: We laugh far less when other people are near.
We laugh about the same whether alone or not.
We are far more likely to laugh around others.
We laugh only when we are completely alone.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ C — ย่อหน้าที่ 2 บอกว่าเรามีโอกาสหัวเราะมากกว่าถึง 30 เท่าเมื่ออยู่กับคนอื่นเทียบกับอยู่คนเดียว

questions.stem: When do babies begin to laugh, according to the passage?
options: Only after they can understand simple jokes.

Once they are able to speak in full sentences.

questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ D — ย่อหน้าที่ 3 ระบุว่าทารกเริ่มหัวเราะราวอายุ 3–4 เดือน ก่อนพูดหรือเข้าใจมุกตลก

questions.stem: In paragraph 2, the phrase "social glue" is closest in meaning to:
options: a sticky substance used in classrooms

questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ B — "social glue" เป็นคำเปรียบเทียบ หมายถึงสิ่งที่เชื่อมผู้คนเข้าด้วยกัน ตรงกับ "something that binds people together"

questions.stem: What can be inferred from laughter-like sounds in chimpanzees and rats?
options: Animals laugh at exactly the same jokes we do.
Only human beings are truly able to laugh.

questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ C — ย่อหน้าที่ 3 บอกว่าพบเสียงคล้ายหัวเราะในชิมแปนซีและหนู จึงอนุมานได้ว่ารากของการหัวเราะเก่าแก่ย้อนไปในวิวัฒนาการ

questions.stem: What happens in the body during laughter?
options: It releases brain chemicals linked to pleasure.

readingLong.title: How Migrating Birds Find Their Way
readingLong.passage:
Every year, billions of birds undertake one of the most astonishing journeys in all of nature. Some travel thousands of kilometres, from the cold north to the warm south and back again, crossing wide oceans, empty deserts, and towering mountains. What amazes scientists most is not the sheer distance, but the accuracy. A small bird may return to the very same garden, year after year, having flown halfway around the world and back. The question is simple, but the answer is not: how on earth do they find their way?

Part of the answer lies in the sun and the stars. Many birds use the position of the sun during the day as a kind of compass, carefully adjusting for the way it moves across the sky from morning to evening. At night, some species navigate by the stars, learning the patterns of the night sky while they are still young in the nest. Like the early human sailors who crossed unknown seas, these birds read the heavens in order to hold their course.

Even more remarkable is the birds' ability to sense the Earth's magnetic field. Our planet behaves like an enormous magnet, and its invisible field points roughly towards the poles. Many birds appear able to detect this field and use it as a built-in compass, allowing them to find the right direction even on cloudy nights when neither the sun nor the stars can be seen. Exactly how they manage this is still not fully understood, but it may involve tiny magnetic particles in their bodies, or even chemical reactions in their eyes that respond to magnetism.

Birds also rely on landmarks and memory. Older, experienced birds appear to remember coastlines, rivers, and mountain ranges, using them as signposts along familiar routes. Smell may play a part too, with some seabirds recognising the particular scent of their home island carried far out on the wind. In other words, birds do not depend on any single method. Instead, they combine several different systems, quietly switching between them as the conditions around them change.

This flexibility helps to explain how migration can be so reliable, yet it also leaves birds surprisingly vulnerable. Bright city lights can confuse birds that are navigating by the stars, and tall buildings and towers often lie directly across their ancient paths. As human beings continue to reshape the planet, scientists increasingly worry that we may be disrupting journeys that have been completed successfully for millions of years.

The navigation of migrating birds remains one of the great wonders of the natural world. That a creature weighing less than a smartphone can cross entire continents and return home, guided by the sun, the stars, and the very magnetism of the Earth, is a powerful reminder of how much skill and intelligence lie hidden in the living world around us.

readingLong.title: The Power of Sleep
readingLong.passage:
We spend about a third of our entire lives asleep, yet for centuries sleep remained one of the great mysteries of science. Why would nature design creatures to spend so many hours unconscious, vulnerable, and unable to find food or defend themselves? For a long time, sleep seemed like nothing more than wasted time. Today, research has revealed the opposite: far from being idle, the sleeping brain is astonishingly busy, and sleep turns out to be one of the most important activities of our entire lives.

One of the clearest discoveries is the link between sleep and memory. While we sleep, the brain replays and reorganises what we learned during the day, moving important information into long-term storage and quietly discarding what does not matter. Students who sleep well after studying remember far more than those who stay awake; in a very real sense, we learn not only while reading, but also while sleeping afterwards. A single late night before an exam can undo hours of hard work.

Sleep also repairs the body. During deep sleep, the body releases hormones that help muscles grow and tissues heal, which is why rest is so important after illness, injury, or hard exercise. The immune system, too, depends on sleep; people who sleep too little catch colds more easily and recover more slowly. Even the brain cleans itself during sleep, washing away waste products that have built up during the day.

Given all this, it is worrying that so many people, especially teenagers, do not get enough sleep. The reasons are familiar: late-night screens, early school start times, homework, and busy lives. Yet the effects are serious. Lack of sleep is linked to poor concentration, low mood, weaker memory, and even long-term health problems. A tired brain struggles to learn, to focus, and to control emotions — exactly the abilities that students most need.

The good news is that better sleep is often within reach. Keeping regular sleeping hours, avoiding bright screens late at night, and making the bedroom dark and quiet can all help a great deal. Short naps can be useful too, as long as they are not taken too late in the day. These small habits cost nothing, yet their effect on health and learning can be truly remarkable. Above all, going to bed and waking up at roughly the same time each day helps the body settle into a steady and healthy rhythm.

Sleep, then, is not a luxury or a sign of laziness, but a basic biological need, as essential as food and water. The hours we spend asleep are not lost; they are an investment, quietly preparing our minds and bodies for everything we hope to do while we are awake. To take sleep seriously is, in the end, to take our own health and learning seriously too.

readingLong.title: How Volcanoes Shape Our World
readingLong.passage:
When most people picture a volcano, they imagine destruction: rivers of glowing lava, clouds of ash, and villages buried beneath the rock. Volcanoes can indeed be deadly, and their dangers should never be underestimated. Yet this is only half of the story. Over millions of years, volcanoes have also shaped our planet in ways that make life itself possible, building new land, enriching the soil, and even helping to create the air we breathe.

Volcanoes form where the hot, partly molten rock beneath the Earth's surface forces its way upward. When this molten rock, called magma, finally reaches the surface, it erupts as lava, ash, and gas. As the lava cools and hardens, it builds new rock and, over time, entirely new land. Many of the world's islands, including whole chains in the middle of the oceans, were created by underwater volcanoes that erupted again and again until they slowly rose above the waves.

Far from ruining the land, volcanoes can also make it much richer. Volcanic ash and rock are full of the minerals that plants need, and over thousands of years they break down into some of the most fertile soil on Earth. This is why, despite the obvious dangers, people have always chosen to farm and to live on the slopes of volcanoes. The very mountains that threaten them also feed them, producing rich harvests that ordinary soil simply cannot match. In many parts of the world, the greenest and most productive fields of all lie quietly in the shadow of a volcano.

Volcanoes have shaped the planet on an even grander scale. Scientists believe that, early in the Earth's history, gases released by countless erupting volcanoes helped to form the atmosphere and the oceans. In other words, the air we breathe and the water we drink owe a debt to volcanic activity that took place long before any living thing existed. Without volcanoes, the Earth might be a very different, and far less welcoming, world.

Today, scientists study volcanoes closely, not only to understand the distant past but to protect people in the present. By measuring small earthquakes, escaping gases, and tiny changes in the shape of a mountain, they can sometimes warn communities before an eruption, giving them precious time to escape. This knowledge does not make volcanoes safe, but it can and does save many lives every year, especially in countries where millions of people live close to active mountains.

Volcanoes, then, are neither simply enemies nor simply friends. They are among the most powerful forces shaping our planet, capable of both terrible destruction and astonishing creation. To understand them is to see the Earth as it truly is: a living, restless world, still being shaped and reshaped beneath our very feet.

readingLong.title: Should We Have a Four-Day Working Week?
readingLong.passage:
For more than a century, the standard working week in many countries has been five days. Recently, however, a growing number of companies and even governments have begun to test a bold alternative: a four-day week, in which employees work one day less but receive exactly the same pay. Supporters call it the future of work; critics call it unrealistic. So which side is right?

Those in favour point to a surprising body of evidence. In several trials around the world, companies that moved to a four-day week reported that productivity stayed the same, or even rose, despite the shorter hours. Workers, they argue, often waste time during long weeks, and a shorter week pushes everyone to focus and to cut out unnecessary meetings. Just as importantly, employees in these trials reported less stress, better health, and far more time for family, rest, and life outside work. Happier, healthier workers, supporters insist, are also better workers.

There are wider benefits too. With one fewer day of commuting, traffic and pollution both fall. Parents gain more time with their children, and people have more hours to exercise, to volunteer, or simply to rest. Some supporters argue that, as machines and computers do more and more of our work, sharing the remaining work over fewer hours is a natural and fair step forward for society.

Critics, however, raise serious doubts. Not every job, they point out, fits neatly into four days. A hospital, a shop, or a factory cannot simply close for an extra day, and squeezing the same amount of work into fewer days may leave some workers more stressed, not less. In jobs where output depends directly on the hours worked, a shorter week may mean lower production or higher costs. There is also the real danger that "four days" quietly becomes four very long and exhausting days instead.

The truth is that the four-day week may suit some industries far better than others. An office where work can be measured by results is very different from a job where someone must be physically present at every moment. Rather than forcing a single rule on everyone, many experts suggest that the real lesson of these trials is broader: that the link between long hours and good work is far weaker than we once believed, and that there may be many different ways to organise work more wisely.

Whether or not the four-day week ever becomes common, the debate itself is valuable. It forces us to ask a question that is easy to forget in our busy lives: not simply how much we work, but why we work, and what kind of life all of that work is actually supposed to support.

questions.stem: What is the central question of the passage?
options: Whether workers should be paid less money.
Whether we should have a four-day working week.
Whether hospitals should close on weekends.
Whether machines should do all of our work.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ B — ทั้งบทถกประเด็น "ควรมีสัปดาห์ทำงาน 4 วันไหม" ตัวเลือกอื่นเป็นรายละเอียดย่อย

questions.stem: What did several trials report about productivity?
options: It fell sharply in every single company.
It could not be measured at all.
It stayed the same or even rose.
It only mattered in large factories.

questions.stem: What wider benefit of a four-day week is mentioned?
options: Less commuting reduces traffic and pollution.

questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ A — ย่อหน้าที่ 3 บอกว่าการเดินทางน้อยลงหนึ่งวันช่วยลดการจราจรและมลพิษ

questions.stem: In paragraph 4, "squeezing the same work into fewer days" — "squeezing" is closest in meaning to:

questions.stem: Why might a four-day week not suit a hospital?
options: Because hospital workers dislike having free time.

questions.stem: In paragraph 5, "a single rule on everyone" refers to applying the four-day week to:

readingLong.title: Should Public Transport Be Free?
readingLong.passage:
In most cities, using a bus or a train means paying a fare. Recently, however, a striking idea has gained attention around the world: what if public transport were completely free, paid for by the government rather than by passengers? A handful of cities have already tried it, and the results have reopened an old debate about how a city should move its people.

Supporters of free transport offer several arguments. The first concerns the environment. If buses and trains cost nothing, they argue, more people will leave their cars at home, reducing traffic, pollution, and the noise that fills modern cities. The second concerns fairness. Transport fares take a far larger share of income from the poor than from the rich, and free transport would help those who most need to reach jobs, schools, and hospitals. Removing fares also speeds up journeys, since buses no longer wait while passengers pay, and it saves the considerable cost of printing tickets and operating machines.

Critics, however, warn that the word "free" is misleading. The money must come from somewhere, usually from taxes, including taxes paid by people who never use public transport at all. They worry that without fare income, transport systems may run short of money for repairs and improvements, leading to older, dirtier, and less reliable services. A free but crumbling bus network, they argue, helps almost no one. Some critics also point out that fares help to manage demand, keeping the busiest services from becoming dangerously overcrowded.

There is also a question of who actually changes their behaviour. Studies of cities that made transport free found that many new passengers were not former drivers, but people who would otherwise have walked or cycled. If that is true, free transport may do less for the environment than its supporters hope, while costing a great deal. Some experts suggest that the same money might achieve far more if it were spent on making services faster and more frequent, rather than simply free.

Between these views lies a more careful position. Free transport may work well in smaller cities, or for certain groups such as students, the elderly, or the poor, without being offered to absolutely everyone. Combined with investment in better routes and cleaner vehicles, such targeted schemes may capture many of the benefits while avoiding the worst of the costs.

In the end, the debate over free transport is about far more than the price of a ticket. It asks what we believe public services are really for, and how a fair society should share both their benefits and their costs. Whether or not buses and trains ever become free, asking the question forces us to think more clearly about the kind of cities we truly want to build.

readingLong.title: Should Students Be Allowed to Use AI for Their Schoolwork?
readingLong.passage:
Within a remarkably short time, powerful artificial intelligence tools have appeared that can write essays, solve problems, and answer almost any question in seconds. For students, these tools are both a temptation and a source of confusion. Schools and universities are now wrestling with a difficult question: should students be allowed to use AI for their schoolwork, and if so, where exactly should the line be drawn?

Those who welcome AI in education point to its potential as a tool for learning. Used well, they argue, AI can act like a patient private tutor, explaining difficult ideas in different ways, offering instant feedback, and helping students who are stuck. It can translate, summarise, and suggest, freeing students to focus on understanding rather than on routine tasks. In the working world that students will soon enter, these tools are already common, so learning to use them wisely, supporters say, is itself an important skill.

Opponents, however, fear that AI may damage learning rather than support it. If a student can produce a finished essay with a single command, they may never develop the ability to think, write, and argue for themselves. Skills are built through effort and struggle, and a tool that removes the struggle may also quietly remove the learning. There is also the problem of honesty: if work produced by a machine is presented as a student's own, how can a teacher judge what the student has actually learned?

A further difficulty is fairness and trust. Students who use AI heavily may earn higher marks than those who do all the work themselves, which seems deeply unfair. Yet detecting AI use is notoriously unreliable, and wrongly accusing innocent students is a serious risk. Banning the tools completely is tempting, but probably impossible, since students can simply use them at home where no one is watching.

Most thoughtful educators are therefore searching for a balanced approach rather than a simple ban or a complete free-for-all. They suggest teaching students when AI is genuinely helpful and when it is not, designing tasks that require real thinking, and being open about what kind of help is allowed. Some assessments may be done in class, without devices, while others may openly invite the use of AI, as long as students explain exactly how they used it. Above all, they want students to see these tools as an aid to thinking, not as a substitute for it.

In the end, the arrival of AI forces schools to ask what education is really for. If it is only about producing finished answers, then machines can already do much of the work. But if it is about developing minds that can think, question, and create, then how students use these powerful new tools — and how honestly — may matter more than ever before.

readingLong.title: Should Schools Start Later in the Morning?
readingLong.passage:
In many countries, the school day begins early, often before eight o'clock in the morning. For generations, this has simply been accepted as normal. Recently, however, scientists and educators have begun to question it, pointing to a growing body of evidence about teenage sleep. Their conclusion is striking: starting school so early may be working against the very students it is meant to help. What once seemed a simple matter of routine has slowly become a genuine scientific question.

The argument rests on biology. During the teenage years, the body's internal clock naturally shifts, making young people feel sleepy later at night and want to wake later in the morning. This is not laziness; it is a real biological change. Yet most schools demand that teenagers wake very early, often after far too little sleep. Tired students, research shows, struggle to concentrate, remember less, and feel more anxious. Forcing them to learn early in the morning, supporters of change argue, is rather like asking them to work in the middle of their natural night.

Several schools that have delayed their start times report encouraging results. Students arrive more rested and alert, attendance improves, and in some cases grades rise. There may be safety benefits too, since sleepy teenagers are more likely to have accidents. Teachers, too, often notice that lessons run more smoothly later in the day. To supporters, the evidence points clearly in one direction: a later start would mean healthier, happier, and more successful students.

Critics, however, raise practical concerns. A later school day means a later finish, which can clash with sports, part-time jobs, and after-school activities. For families, changing school hours can create serious difficulties: parents who must leave early for work may have no one to look after younger children. School buses, often shared between schools at different times, would need to be reorganised, and younger children at the same school may need different timing, adding to the difficulty. Some argue that the cost and disruption outweigh the benefits.

There is also a question of habits. Some critics argue that teenagers stay up late mainly because of phones and screens, not biology, and that the real solution is better sleep habits rather than later school hours. Supporters reply that, while screens certainly play a part, the underlying biological shift is real and cannot simply be switched off by willpower.

In the end, the debate over school start times is about more than timetables. It asks whether schools should be designed around tradition and convenience, or around what we now understand about how young people actually learn and grow. As the evidence continues to build, more and more communities are deciding that the question is at least worth asking seriously.

readingLong.title: How Earthquakes Happen
readingLong.passage:
Without warning, the ground begins to shake. Buildings tremble, objects fall, and in the most severe cases whole cities can be damaged in moments. Earthquakes are among the most frightening and destructive of all natural events. Yet behind their sudden violence lies a slow and steady process, deep beneath our feet, that has been shaping the Earth for billions of years. For thousands of years, people explained these terrifying events with myths and legends, long before science could offer a real answer.

To understand earthquakes, we must look at the outer shell of our planet. The Earth's surface is not a single solid piece, but is broken into enormous sections called tectonic plates, which fit together rather like the pieces of a giant cracked eggshell. These plates are not still. Driven by heat from deep within the Earth, they move very slowly — only a few centimetres a year, about as fast as your fingernails grow — but with truly unimaginable force.

Most earthquakes happen at the edges, where two plates meet. As the plates push against, pull away from, or slide past one another, enormous pressure builds up at the boundary. For years, the rough edges of the plates may lock together, holding firm while the strain steadily increases. Then, suddenly, the rock can take no more. The plates slip, the stored energy is released all at once, and the ground shakes as that energy travels outward in waves. The largest earthquakes can release more energy in a few seconds than a whole city uses in a year.

This is exactly why earthquakes are so hard to predict. The slow build-up of pressure may take decades or even centuries, and the moment of release can come at almost any time. Scientists can identify which areas are most at risk, because they lie along the boundaries between plates, but they still cannot say exactly when an earthquake will strike. This uncertainty makes earthquakes especially dangerous in crowded cities built in risky zones.

Although we cannot prevent earthquakes, we can prepare for them. In countries where earthquakes are common, engineers design buildings to sway and absorb the shaking rather than collapse, and people practise what to do when the ground moves. Such preparations cannot stop an earthquake, but they can mean the difference between a frightening event and a deadly disaster. Simple steps, such as knowing where to shelter, can save lives in the crucial moments before help arrives.

Earthquakes remind us of a truth that is easy to forget: the ground beneath us is not as solid and still as it seems. We live on the thin, moving surface of a restless planet, whose vast forces shaped the mountains and oceans long before we ever existed, and continue quietly to shape them today.
