questions.stem: How do engineers design buildings in earthquake zones?
options: To sway and absorb the shaking.
To be as tall as they possibly can.
To have no windows at all.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ A — ย่อหน้าที่ 5 บอกว่าวิศวกรออกแบบอาคารให้โยกและดูดซับแรงสั่นแทนที่จะพังถล่ม

questions.stem: What is the writer's main purpose?
options: To frighten readers away from all cities.
To prove the Earth is perfectly still.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ D — บทมุ่งอธิบายว่าแผ่นดินไหวเกิดขึ้นได้อย่างไร ไม่ได้ขู่ ขายประกัน หรือบอกว่าโลกนิ่งสนิท

questions.stem: How is the passage mainly organized?
options: It follows one earthquake second by second.
It explains the cause, then prediction and preparation.
It lists earthquakes from strongest to weakest.
It gives detailed steps for building a whole new city.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ B — บทอธิบายสาเหตุ (แผ่นเปลือกโลก) → ความยากในการทำนาย → การเตรียมพร้อม จึงเป็นโครงนี้

readingLong.title: Why the Sky Is Blue
readingLong.passage:
It is one of the first questions many children ask: why is the sky blue? The answer seems obvious until we try to give it, and then we realise we have no idea. After all, the air is not blue; we can see straight through it. Sunlight, too, looks white or yellow, not blue. So where does the colour of the sky actually come from? The explanation, worked out by scientists over a century ago, is a beautiful piece of physics hidden in plain sight above our heads.

The first surprise is that sunlight, which appears white, is in fact a mixture of all the colours of the rainbow. When sunlight passes through a glass prism, or through raindrops, it spreads out into red, orange, yellow, green, blue, and violet. Each colour is really a wave of light, and these waves are not all the same. Red light travels in long, gentle waves, while blue light travels in much shorter, tighter waves.

As sunlight pours into the Earth's atmosphere, it collides with countless tiny molecules of gas in the air. When light hits these molecules, it scatters, bouncing off in all directions. Here is the key fact: shorter waves of light scatter far more strongly than longer ones. Blue light, with its short waves, is scattered all across the sky, while red and yellow light, with their longer waves, mostly travel straight through. As a result, when we look up, we see blue light arriving from every direction at once.

This same simple idea explains another everyday mystery: why sunsets are red. At sunset, the sun is low, and its light must pass through a much thicker layer of atmosphere to reach our eyes. Along this long path, almost all of the blue light is scattered away long before it arrives. What remains is the red and orange light, which travels more easily through the air — and so the setting sun glows in warm, fiery colours.

It is remarkable that something as ordinary as the colour of the sky hides such an elegant explanation. The blue overhead and the red of the sunset are not really properties of the air itself, but the result of sunlight, waves, and tiny molecules interacting in ways our eyes can see but never directly notice. The science was first explained in detail in the nineteenth century, and it still amazes those who learn it for the first time.

The next time you look up at a clear blue sky, or watch the sun sink in a blaze of orange, you are not simply seeing the weather. You are watching physics in action — a daily, silent display of how light behaves, performed above our heads from the moment the sun rises until long after it sets.

questions.stem: What is the passage mainly about?
options: How children learn to ask questions.
Why the air is full of gas molecules.
Why the sky is blue and sunsets are red.
How to use a glass prism at home.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ C — ทั้งบทอธิบายว่าทำไมท้องฟ้าจึงเป็นสีฟ้าและพระอาทิตย์ตกจึงเป็นสีแดง ส่วน A/B/D ไม่ใช่ใจความหลัก

questions.stem: What is white sunlight really made of?
options: A mixture of all the colours of the rainbow.
A single colour that never changes.
Only the two colours red and orange together.
A kind of invisible blue gas.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ A — ย่อหน้าที่ 2 บอกว่าแสงอาทิตย์สีขาวจริงๆ เป็นส่วนผสมของทุกสีในสายรุ้ง

questions.stem: How does blue light differ from red light?
options: Blue light cannot travel through air.
Blue light is much warmer than red.
Blue light moves far more slowly.
Blue light travels in much shorter waves.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ D — ย่อหน้าที่ 2 บอกว่าแสงสีแดงเป็นคลื่นยาว ส่วนแสงสีฟ้าเป็นคลื่นสั้นกว่ามาก

questions.stem: In paragraph 3, the word "scatters" is closest in meaning to:
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ B — "it scatters, bouncing off in all directions" ดังนั้น scatter = กระเจิง/สะท้อนไปทุกทิศ ตรงกับ "bounces off in all directions"

questions.stem: Why do we see blue when we look up?
options: Because the air itself is painted blue.
Because red light scatters most strongly.
Because blue light scatters across the whole sky.
Because the sun gives off only blue light.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ C — ย่อหน้าที่ 3 บอกว่าแสงสีฟ้าคลื่นสั้นกระเจิงไปทั่วท้องฟ้า เราจึงเห็นสีฟ้ามาจากทุกทิศเมื่อแหงนมอง

questions.stem: Why are sunsets red?
options: Blue light is scattered away on its long path.
The sun gives off only red light at night.
The air becomes much thinner at sunset.
Red light scatters more than any other colour.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ A — ย่อหน้าที่ 4 บอกว่าตอนตะวันตก แสงผ่านชั้นบรรยากาศหนาขึ้น แสงสีฟ้าถูกกระเจิงหายไปก่อน เหลือแสงสีแดง/ส้ม

questions.stem: What is the writer's main purpose?
options: To warn readers not to look at the sun.
To sell special glass prisms to young children.
To prove that the air is coloured blue.
To explain the science behind the sky's colours.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ D — บทมุ่งอธิบายฟิสิกส์เบื้องหลังสีของท้องฟ้า ไม่ได้เตือนภัย ขายปริซึม หรือบอกว่าอากาศมีสีฟ้า

questions.stem: How is the passage mainly organized?
options: It tells one sunset minute by minute.
It poses a question, then explains the physics.
It lists colours from brightest to darkest.
It gives steps for building a prism.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ B — บทตั้งคำถาม (ทำไมท้องฟ้าฟ้า) แล้วอธิบายหลักฟิสิกส์ (แสง คลื่น การกระเจิง) จึงเป็นโครงคำถาม–คำอธิบาย

readingLong.title: Should Exams Be Replaced by Coursework?
readingLong.passage:
For generations, the final exam has been the standard way of measuring what students have learned. Yet exams have always had their critics, and recently the debate has grown louder. Some argue that traditional exams should be replaced, or at least balanced, by coursework — projects, essays, and assignments completed over time. Which approach really gives the truest picture of a student's ability?

Those who favour coursework point to the weaknesses of exams. A single test, taken on a single day, may not reflect what a student truly knows. Nervous students can freeze, and a moment of bad luck — illness, a difficult night's sleep, an unfamiliar question — can ruin months of hard work. A student who understands a subject deeply may still panic and underperform on the day. Coursework, by contrast, allows students to show their understanding gradually, to think deeply, to research, and to improve their work, much as people do in real jobs. It rewards steady effort rather than performance under pressure.

Supporters of exams, however, defend them firmly. An exam, they argue, is fair precisely because every student faces the same questions, in the same conditions, at the same time, with no outside help. Coursework, they warn, is far easier to cheat at: a student may receive help from parents, friends, or, increasingly, from technology, making it hard to know whose work is really being marked. Exams also teach valuable skills — working under pressure, managing time, and thinking quickly — that matter throughout life.

There is also the question of practicality. Exams are relatively quick and cheap to organise and to mark, while good coursework takes a great deal of teachers' time to set, supervise, and assess fairly. In large schools with limited resources, replacing every exam with carefully judged coursework could place an enormous burden on already busy teachers.

Faced with these competing strengths, many education systems have chosen not to pick one or the other, but to combine them. A final grade might rest partly on coursework completed during the year and partly on an exam at the end, so that students are judged both on steady effort and on what they can do under pressure. In this way, the weaknesses of each method are balanced by the strengths of the other. Such mixed systems try to be fair to many different kinds of learner at once.

In the end, the debate reflects a deeper question: what are we really trying to measure? If we want to test memory and quick thinking, exams may serve well; if we value research, creativity, and persistence, coursework may be better. Perhaps the wisest schools are those that remember no single method can capture everything a young person is capable of becoming.

questions.stem: What is the central question of the passage?
options: Whether students should study at night.
Whether exams should be replaced by coursework.
Whether teachers are paid enough money.
Whether essays are longer than tests.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ B — ทั้งบทถกประเด็น "ควรเปลี่ยนจากการสอบเป็นงานที่ทำต่อเนื่อง (coursework) ไหม" ตัวเลือกอื่นเป็นรายละเอียดย่อย

questions.stem: What weakness of exams do their critics point to?
options: They are far too easy for everyone.
They take many months to complete.
A single bad day can ruin months of work.
They reward research and deep thinking.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ C — ย่อหน้าที่ 2 บอกว่าการสอบวันเดียวอาจไม่สะท้อนความรู้จริง โชคร้ายวันเดียว (ป่วย/นอนไม่พอ) ทำลายความพยายามหลายเดือนได้

questions.stem: Why do supporters say exams are fair?
options: Every student faces the same questions and conditions.
Students can get help from their friends as well.
They usually take a very long time to mark.
They openly allow the use of any technology.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ A — ย่อหน้าที่ 3 บอกว่าการสอบยุติธรรมเพราะทุกคนเจอคำถามเดียวกัน เงื่อนไขเดียวกัน เวลาเดียวกัน โดยไม่มีตัวช่วย

questions.stem: In paragraph 4, the word "burden" is closest in meaning to:
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ D — "an enormous burden on already busy teachers" หมายถึงภาระหนักที่ครูต้องแบก ตรงกับ "a heavy load to carry"

questions.stem: Why is coursework described as easier to cheat at?
options: Because it is always done inside an exam hall.
Because students may get outside help at home.
Because teachers complete it for the students.
Because it is never marked by anyone.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ B — ย่อหน้าที่ 3 บอกว่า coursework โกงง่ายกว่า เพราะนักเรียนอาจได้รับความช่วยเหลือจากพ่อแม่ เพื่อน หรือเทคโนโลยีที่บ้าน

questions.stem: What practical advantage do exams have?
options: They reward steady effort over time.
They allow students to improve their work.
They are relatively quick and cheap to mark.
They require no questions at all.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ C — ย่อหน้าที่ 4 บอกว่าการสอบจัดและตรวจได้ค่อนข้างเร็วและประหยัด ต่างจาก coursework ที่กินเวลาครูมาก

questions.stem: In paragraph 5, "the strengths of the other" refers to the strengths of:
options: the other method, whether exam or coursework
the students who actually sit the test
the parents who are helping out at home
the teachers who mark the work
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ A — ประโยคบอกว่าจุดอ่อนของวิธีหนึ่งถูกชดเชยด้วยจุดแข็งของอีกวิธี ดังนั้น the other จึงหมายถึงอีกวิธีหนึ่ง (สอบ หรือ coursework)

questions.stem: How would you best describe the writer's overall position?
options: Certain that exams should be abolished completely.
Sure that coursework can never be trusted.
Uninterested in how students are tested.
Balanced, supporting a combination of both methods.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ D — ผู้เขียนเสนอทั้งสองฝ่ายแล้วเอนไปทางการผสมทั้งสองวิธี จึงเป็นจุดยืนสมดุล

questions.stem: How is the passage mainly organized?
options: It tells one student's whole exam day in order.
It gives arguments for, against, then a combined approach.
It lists school subjects by their difficulty.
It explains, step by step, how to write a long essay.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ B — บทเสนอฝ่ายหนุน coursework → ฝ่ายหนุนสอบ → เรื่องการปฏิบัติจริง → แล้วสรุปแนวทางผสม จึงเป็นโครงนี้
